title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
Insurance Nominee
Overview Of Life Insurance Nominee
A life insurance policy usually covers the risk of early demise of the life insured. It pays a death benefit if the life assured happens to pass away during the policy tenure. But who receives the death benefit if the life assured passes away?
Nomination is defined under Section 39 of the Insurance Act1. Simply put, a nominee is an individual or entity named by the Policyholder / Life Assured to receive the death benefit under the policy1.
Remember that nomination is not equivalent to the beneficiary. An insurance nominee can receive the death benefit and hold it as a trustee10. The legal heirs can claim as per their succession8.
For instance, say Mr. Sharma nominates his mother in his life insurance policy. However, in his Will, he specifies that the policy benefits should be used by his children. In this case, the mother is the insurance nominee who will receive the death benefit as a trustee. Mr Sharma’s children will be the rightful beneficiaries.
Do You Need A Nominee While Buying Life Insurance?
Nomination has become mandatory11 when you buy a life insurance policy3. This is because having a nominee eases the settlement of death claims.
Without an insurance nominee, a Succession Certificate, probate of a registered will or a Legal Heir Certificate9 are needed to pay the benefit to the legal heirs. This might take time. However, when the nominees are named, insurers can easily pay in favour of the nominated individual. The nominee’s work is to manage the money received till it is legally transferred to the legal heirs10.
Thus, legal mandate aside, having an insurance nominee is beneficial for policy payments. This helps you transfer the payout to the individual you want. Moreover, since nomination can be changed, you can choose the individual to receive the policy proceeds whenever you want to. You can also change the nominee if your preference changes.
How To Choose A Nominee?
There are no specific guidelines on who can be a life insurance policy nominee. However, it is preffered that you opt for a beneficial nominee11 at the time of buying a life insurance policy so that the funds can be utilised immediately and the claim is undisputed.There is a special category called “Beneficial Nominee” that has been added to the Insurance Act wherein it includes spouse, children and parents11 which is preferred unlike regular nominees like siblings, etc.
If you want to appoint a minor as the insurance nominee, you need to choose an appointee1 too till he/she is not 18 years old. Thus, you can nominate your minor child, grandchildren, etc. also to receive the benefits.
Types Of Nominees
A life insurance policy has various types of nominees. Some of them are as follows –
Beneficial Nominee3The beneficial nominee is an individual who is also the beneficiary of the policy, i.e., an individual entitled to use the policy proceeds for his needs. The claim of the policy is paid to the beneficial nominee, who can utilise the money as he/she sees fit. Usually, parents, spouses, and children are considered beneficial nominees11 for a life insurance policy. The life assured does not have to instruct the insurance company regarding the type of nominee if he nominates any of these individuals since they are automatically considered beneficial nominees.
MinorA nominee can be a minor. In such cases, the life assured needs to appoint an appointee1 to collect the policy proceeds on behalf of the minor nominee.
Secondary Nominee13In case the primary nominee is not alive at the time of filing the claim, the secondary nominee can claim the same.
Can a non-family member5 be a nominee? A non-family nominee is not related to the life assured. It can be a friend or acquaintance of the life assured. However, in the case of non-family nominees, the insurance company may perceive a moral hazard. It will question the nominee's choice, and if it suspects any fraudulent or unscrupulous reason for nominating a non-family member, it might not allow such a nomination.If you wish to pass on the benefits of your life insurance policy to anyone other than your beneficial nominee, it is best to assign the policy in their name, so that the claim would be undispited12.
Top 4 Things To Keep In Mind At The Time Of Selecting A Nominee For Your Life Insurance Plan:
When buying a life insurance plan and selecting a nominee, here are some things that you should consider -
Relationship with the NomineeAssess your relationship with the proposed insurance nominee. If the nominee is your spouse, your children or your dependent parents, they can become a beneficial nominee3 and use the death benefit for their financial needs.
Age of the Nominee
If the nominee is very old, they might predecease you, so you will have to change the nominee. On the other hand, if the nominee is a minor, you would have to appoint another individual as the appointee. So, consider the nominee’s age when appointing them as the receiver of the policy proceeds.
Financial Dependents:See who depends on you financially and nominate those individuals so that they can become beneficial nominees and use the policy proceeds when you are not around.
Number of Nominees
You can choose multiple nominees if you have multiple individuals depending on you financially. However, specify the percentage of claims payable to each in such cases.
Is Life Insurance Premium Tax Deductible?
Yes, the premium payable for a life insurance policy can be claimed as a deduction from the taxable income under Section 80C6 of the Income Tax Act 1961 if you choose the old regime for tax filing. The total limit of deduction available under Section 80C is ₹1.5 lakhs6.
However, some conditions should be fulfilled to claim the deduction on the premium paid. These conditions are as follows6 –
For policies bought on or after 1st April 2012, a premium paid up to 10% of the capital sum assured will be the allowed deduction.
If you bought the policy before 31 March 2012, you can deduct a premium paid up to 20% of the capital sum assured.
For policies bought on or after 1 April 2013, if you have any disease or disablement mentioned in Section 80U or 80DDB, a premium up to 15% of the capital sum assured will be the allowed deduction.
Premium paid by individuals and Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs) are eligible for tax deduction.
You can pay the premium for a policy taken on your life or the life of your spouse and children. In the case of HUFs, the policy should be applied to the life of any member of the HUF.
If you buy a Unit Linked Insurance Plan (ULIP) plan, you should continue to hold the policy for the next 5 years. Early surrender would reverse the premium deduction.
In the case of other life insurance policies, you should only surrender the life insurance policy after 2 years, or else the deduction would reverse.
Does The Nominee Of Life Insurance Pay Taxes?
An insurance nominee is entitled to receive only the death benefit of a life insurance policy. The death benefit is always tax-free7. As such, the nominee does not have to pay any tax on the life insurance money received.
How Does The Process Of Nomination Work In The Case Of Life Insurance?
Nomination under a policy is simple. It forms part of the Proposal Process and after that, if you wish to change, simply write to your insurance company. Minor forms may be filled out to do the same, but it does not take more than a few minutes. You can also visit the insurer’s mobile application and change the nomination.
The life assured can nominate one or multiple individuals as nominees. For multiple nominees13, the proportion of the death claim payable to each has to be specified. For instance, if an individual nominates his wife and children as nominees, he must specify the percentage of claim payable to each. The choice of allocation depends entirely on the life assured.
You can nominate an individual either when buying the policy or later on. The nominee can also be changed during the policy tenure any number of times13.
If the insurance nominee is a minor, the life assured should appoint an appointee1. The appointee should be an adult who will receive the death benefit on behalf of the minor nominee if the life assured passes away when the nominee is still a minor. If the nominee reaches adulthood during the policy tenure and the life assured is alive, the appointee becomes invalid, and the minor is considered the rightful nominee to receive the policy benefits1.
For instance, say Mr Verma appoints his 5-year-old son as the nominee and his wife as the appointee in the policy. If Mr Verma passes away within the next 13 years when his son is still a minor, his wife will receive the death benefit on behalf of his son. On the other hand, if Mr. Verma survives 13 years and his son reaches adulthood, he becomes the rightful nominee. If Mr Verma passes away, later on, the son will receive the death benefit.
If the nominee passes away before the life assured, the life assured should nominate another individual in the place of the deceased nominee. If no one is nominated and the life assured also passes away, the legal heirs of the insured can claim the policy2.
Difference Between Nominee and Beneficiary
As mentioned earlier, an insurance nominee is not considered the beneficiary of the policy unless he is a beneficial nominee. A nominee is entitled to collect the policy proceeds while a beneficiary is the end user of the proceeds. If a beneficial nominee is added, the nominee also becomes the beneficiary, but if not, the following table shows how a nominee is different from a beneficiary –
To avoid this confusion, insurance companies have added the section of “beneficial nominee” to ensure that the claim is undisputed11.
Conclusion
When buying a life insurance policy, nominate an individual to receive the policy payout in the case of the demise of the life insured. Know the rules of nomination and the rights that a nominee has. You can also change the insurance nominee if you wish, but not appointing one is not a choice. Since nomination is mandatory, choose the right nominee in your life insurance policy so that your family receives financial assistance when needed.
FAQs
Can I choose an NRI, i.e., a non-resident Indian, as my nominee for a life insurance policy?
Yes, an NRI can be a nominee in a life insurance policy. As long as the process of claim is followed, it is same for a resident Indian or an NRI14.
What is the necessary documentation that is required to appoint a nominee for a life insurance policy?
No documents are needed to appoint a nominee to a life insurance policy. You just have to name the individual (s) you want to nominate and the nomination percentage, i.e., the percentage of claim payable to the appointed nominee (in case of multiple nominees). The relevant documents need to be provided by the nominee at the time of claim.
What happens if a nominee dies before the policyholder in a life insurance policy?
If the nominee predeceases the life assured, another nominee can be nominated instead of the previous one. However, if the life assured does not change the nominee, the legal heirs can claim if the life assured passes away.
